refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable the operator (on-site or remote) to execute earthmoving tasks with measurable productivity of ≥ productivity_target (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations over the agreed evaluation period, while maintaining compliance with worksite safety plan and operational policies.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-001 is a high-level acceptance criterion that conflates productivity measurement with operational capability. Refinement separates the measurement/verification aspect from the capability delivery aspect. The refined FR explicitly acknowledges dual operator modes (on-site and tele-operated), incorporates the safety and policy constraints as boundary conditions, and defines the measurable outcome (≥ target over evaluation period) as the verification hook.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct representative operations trial over agreed period (e.g., 40 operating hours). Log task cycles, material moved (m³ or ton), elapsed time, and safety/policy compliance events. Calculate productivity = total_material / total_time. Compare against productivity_target. Verify zero safety plan violations and operational policy breaches.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-001.1: The vehicle shall execute earthmoving task cycles (dig, load, transport, dump) with repeatable cycle time ≤ cycle_time_target.
FR-NGHE-001.2: The operator interface (on-site or remote) shall provide sufficient situational awareness and control authority to command task execution safely and effectively.
FR-NGHE-001.3: The NGHE solution shall detect and alert on safety-critical conditions (e.g., stability, proximity, load state) in real-time to prevent policy violations.
FR-NGHE-001.4: The teleoperation subsystem (if deployed) shall maintain command-response latency ≤ latency_threshold and network availability ≥ availability_threshold to sustain operator control.
FR-NGHE-001.5: The NGHE solution shall log and report productivity metrics (cycle count, material moved, time) for post-evaluation analysis.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Integrate a high-performance autonomous earthmoving vehicle with AI-driven task planning, real-time safety monitoring, and optional tele-operation overlay; measure productivity via onboard telemetry logging.

DP-B: Deploy a human-operated vehicle with enhanced ergonomics, visibility, and safety interlocks; augment with remote monitoring and advisory alerts; measure productivity via manual time-study and material tracking.

DP-C: Hybrid approach: baseline human-operated vehicle with progressive automation (e.g., load-sensing, stability assist, cycle-time optimization); tele-operation as fallback for hazardous zones; integrated telemetry for productivity measurement.

DP-D: Fully tele-operated vehicle with high-bandwidth network link, low-latency command-response, and redundant safety monitoring; operator in remote control center; productivity measured via onboard sensors and network-transmitted telemetry.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid approach—baseline human-operated vehicle with progressive automation (load-sensing, stability assist, cycle-time optimization), tele-operation as fallback for hazardous zones, and integrated telemetry for productivity measurement. This DP balances operator familiarity, safety margin, and productivity gain while maintaining fallback capability and reducing network dependency risk.

potential_sources: Automation logic (cycle-time optimization) is coupled to vehicle dynamics and load-sensing hardware; requires co-design of control algorithms and sensor placement.
Tele-operation fallback is coupled to network link availability and latency; requires redundancy and graceful degradation strategy.
Safety monitoring (stability, proximity) is coupled to both on-site and remote operator awareness; requires unified alert architecture.
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable via clear interface contracts (e.g., sensor data format, command protocol, alert taxonomy). No fundamental contradiction detected. DP-C avoids the extreme coupling of full autonomy (DP-A) and the measurement complexity of manual tracking (DP-B).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Operator training and acceptance of progressive automation is feasible within project timeline.
Assumption: Network infrastructure for tele-operation fallback is available or can be provisioned at representative worksites.
Risk: Cycle-time optimization algorithms may not generalize across different soil conditions, load types, or terrain; requires field tuning.
Risk: Tele-operation latency may exceed operator comfort threshold in high-precision tasks (e.g., loading into confined spaces); fallback to on-site operation may be necessary.
Risk: Safety alert fatigue if automation generates excessive false positives; requires careful tuning of thresholds.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) automation scope (e.g., load-sensing and stability assist enabled by default; cycle-time optimization as optional mode), (2) tele-operation trigger conditions (e.g., operator request, hazard zone entry, on-site control loss), (3) network redundancy strategy (e.g., dual links, local fallback), and (4) safety alert taxonomy and thresholds. This will reduce ambiguity and support clearer IM assignment.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution IM is the top-level system integration boundary that orchestrates the Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules. It owns the overall productivity measurement pipeline, operator mode selection logic (on-site vs. remote), and cross-module coordination. The NGHE Solution IM is responsible for ensuring that the selected DP (hybrid human-operated with progressive automation and tele-operation fallback) is realized as a cohesive, measurable system.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives task definitions from Site Manager or Tele-operator (e.g., 'excavate zone A, load into truck B'). Tasking includes task ID, location, material type, and priority. NGHE Solution parses tasking and distributes to Vehicle and Teleoperation modules.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits productivity metrics (cycle count, material moved, cycle time, operator mode, automation state) and vehicle state (position, load, stability, fuel) to Site Manager and Maintenance/Quality for real-time monitoring and post-evaluation analysis.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Broadcasts safety-critical alerts (stability warning, proximity alert, load overage, policy violation) to Operator, Tele-operator, and Safety Manager. Alerts trigger operator action or automatic intervention (e.g., load shedding, speed reduction).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication channel for tele-operation commands and telemetry uplink. NGHE Solution manages network link health, latency monitoring, and fallback logic (e.g., switch to on-site control if link degrades).

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level integration boundary. It owns the DP realization (hybrid operation, automation, tele-operation fallback, productivity measurement). Minimal diffusion: only cross-module coordination logic and operator mode selection reside here. All domain-specific logic (vehicle control, tele-operation protocol, safety rules) is delegated to child modules.

Vehicle module owns the physical/mechanical realization and on-site operator interface. However, productivity measurement (telemetry logging, cycle counting) and safety alert generation require coordination with Safety Services and NGHE Solution. Tele-operation commands flow through NGHE Solution, not directly to Vehicle. Diffusion risk: if Vehicle owns telemetry logging, it duplicates logic that Safety Services may also need.

Teleoperation module owns remote operator interface and command protocol. However, it does not own the vehicle dynamics or safety rules; these are in Vehicle and Safety Services. Tele-operation is a fallback mode, not the primary path. Diffusion risk: if Teleoperation owns safety alert generation for remote operator, it duplicates Safety Services logic. If Teleoperation owns productivity measurement, it conflicts with NGHE Solution's measurement pipeline.

Safety Services owns safety rule evaluation, alert generation, and policy compliance checking. It is a natural home for real-time safety monitoring. However, it does not own the vehicle control or operator interface; these are in Vehicle and Teleoperation. Diffusion risk: if Safety Services owns productivity measurement, it overloads the module with non-safety concerns.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution IM is the correct top-level boundary because: (1) it owns the DP realization strategy (hybrid operation, automation, tele-operation fallback), (2) it orchestrates the Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules via well-defined interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link), (3) it owns the productivity measurement pipeline (cycle counting, material tracking, time logging), and (4) it manages operator mode selection and fallback logic. Child modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are cohesive, non-overlapping, and support clear ownership boundaries.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a 'god object' if it owns too much coordination logic. Mitigation: define clear delegation rules (e.g., Vehicle owns on-site control, Teleoperation owns remote control, Safety Services owns rule evaluation).

Risk: Productivity measurement may be distributed across Vehicle (cycle counting), Teleoperation (remote mode tracking), and Safety Services (alert-based adjustments). Mitigation: NGHE Solution owns the measurement pipeline and aggregates data from child modules via Telemetry interface.

Risk: Network Link interface may be owned by Teleoperation, but NGHE Solution needs to monitor link health for fallback logic. Mitigation: Teleoperation exposes link health status via Telemetry; NGHE Solution subscribes to it.

Risk: Safety alert generation may be duplicated if both Vehicle and Safety Services generate alerts. Mitigation: Safety Services is the single source of truth for safety alerts; Vehicle reports raw sensor data to Safety Services, which evaluates rules and generates alerts.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the ownership of productivity measurement: (1) Vehicle logs raw cycle events (dig start, dig end, load start, load end, dump start, dump end) and sensor data (load weight, position). (2) NGHE Solution aggregates cycle events and computes productivity metrics (cycle time, material moved, cycles per hour). (3) Telemetry interface carries both raw events and computed metrics to Site Manager. This avoids duplication and ensures a single source of truth for productivity measurement.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP (hybrid human-operated with progressive automation and tele-operation fallback) directly addresses the refined FR (enable operator to execute tasks with measurable productivity ≥ target while maintaining safety and policy compliance). The DP provides multiple pathways to achieve productivity (on-site operation with automation assist, tele-operation fallback for hazardous zones) and includes integrated telemetry for measurement. No fundamental mismatch detected.

diffusion_risks: Productivity measurement logic may be scattered across Vehicle (cycle event logging), NGHE Solution (metric aggregation), and Telemetry interface (data transmission). Mitigation: establish clear ownership (NGHE Solution owns aggregation pipeline).
Safety alert generation may involve both Vehicle (sensor data collection) and Safety Services (rule evaluation). Mitigation: Safety Services is the single source of truth; Vehicle is a data provider.

Tele-operation fallback logic may be split between NGHE Solution (mode selection) and Teleoperation (command protocol). Mitigation: NGHE Solution owns mode selection; Teleoperation owns protocol details.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate top-level IM boundary. It owns the DP realization and orchestrates child modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) via well-defined interfaces. No module is overloaded with unrelated concerns. Interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are clear and non-overlapping.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: The refined FR is verifiable via representative operations trial: log task cycles, material moved, time, and safety/policy compliance events. Compute productivity = total_material / total_time. Compare against target. Verify zero safety violations. The NGHE Solution IM owns the measurement pipeline (Telemetry interface), so data collection is feasible.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The refined FR is allocated to NGHE Solution IM, which owns the DP realization and measurement pipeline. Sub-FRs are allocated to child modules: FR-NGHE-001.1 (cycle time) → Vehicle, FR-NGHE-001.2 (operator interface) → Vehicle + Teleoperation, FR-NGHE-001.3 (safety alerts) → Safety Services, FR-NGHE-001.4 (tele-operation latency) → Teleoperation, FR-NGHE-001.5 (productivity logging) → NGHE Solution. Allocation is clear and non-overlapping.

recommended_revisions: Refine the DP to specify automation scope, tele-operation trigger conditions, network redundancy strategy, and safety alert taxonomy. This will reduce ambiguity in IM assignment and child-level decomposition.
Clarify ownership of productivity measurement: Vehicle logs raw events, NGHE Solution aggregates metrics, Telemetry carries results. This avoids duplication and ensures a single source of truth.
Define interface contracts (e.g., Tasking schema, Telemetry data format, SafetyAlert taxonomy, Network Link protocol) to support clear module boundaries and integration testing.
Establish fallback logic for tele-operation: define trigger conditions (e.g., operator request, hazard zone entry, link degradation), timeout thresholds, and graceful degradation strategy.

decision.justification: The refined FR, selected DP, and assigned IM are coherent and pass all gates. The NGHE Solution IM is the correct top-level boundary, and the sub-FRs are well-defined and ready for decomposition. However, the DP requires refinement (automation scope, tele-operation triggers, network redundancy, safety alert taxonomy) before proceeding to detailed design. Recommend a brief DP refinement cycle (ZIGZAG) to clarify these details, then proceed to decompose sub-FRs (ZIGZAG_DOWN).
decision.justification_detail: ZIGZAG_DOWN is chosen because: (1) the FR-DP-IM alignment is sound, (2) the sub-FRs are well-scoped and ready for next-level decomposition, (3) the IM boundary (NGHE Solution) is appropriate and non-overlapping with child modules, and (4) the interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are clear. However, the DP (hybrid operation, progressive automation, tele-operation fallback) requires clarification on automation scope, tele-operation trigger conditions, network redundancy, and safety alert taxonomy. Recommend a brief DP refinement (ZIGZAG) to address these details, then proceed to decompose sub-FRs at the next level (ZIGZAG_DOWN).
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-001.1: The vehicle shall execute earthmoving task cycles (dig, load, transport, dump) with repeatable cycle time ≤ cycle_time_target.
FR-NGHE-001.2: The operator interface (on-site or remote) shall provide sufficient situational awareness and control authority to command task execution safely and effectively.
FR-NGHE-001.3: The NGHE solution shall detect and alert on safety-critical conditions (e.g., stability, proximity, load state) in real-time to prevent policy violations.
FR-NGHE-001.4: The teleoperation subsystem (if deployed) shall maintain command-response latency ≤ latency_threshold and network availability ≥ availability_threshold to sustain operator control.
FR-NGHE-001.5: The NGHE solution shall log and report productivity metrics (cycle count, material moved, time) for post-evaluation analysis.

revise.why: The selected DP (hybrid human-operated with progressive automation and tele-operation fallback) is sound in concept but requires clarification on: (1) automation scope (which functions are automated, which are operator-controlled), (2) tele-operation trigger conditions (when fallback is activated), (3) network redundancy strategy (how link degradation is handled), and (4) safety alert taxonomy and thresholds (which conditions trigger alerts, at what severity). These details are necessary to support clear IM assignment and child-level decomposition. Recommend a brief DP refinement cycle to address these points, then proceed to ZIGZAG_DOWN.

assumptions: Operator training and acceptance of progressive automation is feasible within project timeline.
Network infrastructure for tele-operation fallback is available or can be provisioned at representative worksites.
Productivity target (m³/h or ton/h) is defined by customer and is achievable with the selected DP.
Safety plan and operational policies are documented and can be encoded as safety rules in Safety Services module.
Representative operations trial can be conducted over agreed period (e.g., 40 operating hours) with sufficient data collection.
Cycle-time optimization algorithms can be tuned to generalize across different soil conditions, load types, and terrain.
open_risks: Cycle-time optimization may not generalize across different soil conditions and terrain; requires field tuning and validation.
Tele-operation latency may exceed operator comfort threshold in high-precision tasks; fallback to on-site operation may be necessary.
Safety alert fatigue if automation generates excessive false positives; requires careful tuning of thresholds.
Network link availability and latency may not meet tele-operation requirements at all representative worksites; may require infrastructure investment.
Operator acceptance of automation and tele-operation fallback may be lower than expected; requires change management and training.
Productivity measurement accuracy depends on reliable cycle event logging and material tracking; sensor failures or calibration drift may introduce errors.
evidence_needed: Customer-defined productivity target (m³/h or ton/h) and evaluation period.
Worksite safety plan and operational policies (documented requirements).
Network infrastructure assessment at representative worksites (link availability, latency, redundancy options).
Operator feedback on automation scope and tele-operation fallback (acceptance, comfort, training needs).
Cycle-time optimization algorithm performance across different soil conditions and terrain (field trial data).
Safety alert threshold tuning results (false positive rate, operator response time).
Productivity measurement accuracy validation (sensor calibration, cycle event logging reliability).
Tele-operation latency and network availability measurements during representative operations trial.
